title: Short Stature in a 13-Year-Old Boy — Navigating CDGP vs Growth Hormone Deficiency
sourceFile: user-provided content (chat) — resident training module (Anant Raj Mishra, Patient #31)

relatedChapters.title: Disorders of Growth and Development: Clinical Perspectives
Disorders of Growth and Development: Diagnosis and Treatment

items.title: How to navigate Anant Raj Mishra's case — height below the 5th percentile at age 13
items.content: Master Anant Raj Mishra, 13 years old, is under evaluation for short stature — his height has fallen below the 5th percentile on the growth chart. The core diagnostic task is tracking growth velocity over time to distinguish constitutional delay of growth and puberty (CDGP) from true growth hormone (GH) deficiency (GHD), with a plan to consider GH therapy only if growth velocity remains poor after structured observation and appropriate testing. At age 13 in a boy, this is the critical peripubertal window where premature GH stimulation testing without sex-steroid priming over-diagnoses deficiency, and where a single height measurement cannot determine the diagnosis — serial velocity is the pivot.
keyPoints: 13y boy, height <5th percentile — short stature confirmed.
Core fork: CDGP vs true GHD — growth velocity over time is the pivot.
GH therapy only if velocity remains poor after observation + appropriate testing.

items.title: Why height below the 5th percentile triggers evaluation but does not diagnose the cause
items.content: Short stature is typically defined as height below the 3rd percentile (approximately −2 SD) or below the 5th percentile depending on local practice — Anant's height below the 5th percentile meets the screening threshold. However, a single centile position does not distinguish familial short stature (genetic, benign), CDGP (delayed maturation, eventual normal adult height), or pathological GHD (true pituitary failure). The next steps are: calculate height SDS, plot at least 12 months of prior heights, compute growth velocity, assess pubertal status and bone age, and compare to mid-parental target height. A boy tracking parallel to his curve at a low centile with delayed bone age and delayed puberty favours CDGP; a boy with decelerating velocity and biochemical evidence of GH insufficiency favours GHD.
keyPoints: <5th percentile = evaluate; does not specify the cause.
Need: height SDS, ≥12 months heights, velocity, puberty, bone age, target height.
Parallel low curve + delayed BA/puberty → CDGP; decelerating + low GH axis → GHD.

items.title: Why growth velocity is the single most important variable in this case
items.content: Growth velocity (cm/year) measured over at least 6–12 months is more informative than any single height. Normal prepubertal velocity in mid-childhood is approximately 5–6 cm/year, slowing to ~4–5 cm/year before the pubertal spurt. CDGP classically maintains a low-normal, parallel velocity (~4 cm/year) — the child tracks along a low centile without crossing downward. True GHD typically produces decelerating velocity — often <4 cm/year and falling further below the curve. A velocity of 3 cm/year or less over two consecutive measurement periods is a red flag for organic pathology (GHD, hypothyroidism, chronic disease, celiac) and is inconsistent with benign CDGP alone. For Anant: measure standing height with a stadiometer every 3–4 months, plot velocity, and do not commit to GH therapy until velocity is documented as persistently poor.
keyPoints: Velocity over ≥6–12 months > single height measurement.
CDGP: parallel tracking, low-normal velocity ~4 cm/yr.
GHD: decelerating velocity, often <4 cm/yr — red flag if ≤3 cm/yr.

items.title: How constitutional delay of growth and puberty presents in a 13-year-old boy
items.content: CDGP is the commonest cause of short stature with delayed puberty in adolescent boys — a variant of normal maturation with late activation of the HPG and somatotropic axes. Features: height below average but often tracking parallel to a low centile (not falling); bone age delayed 2 or more years behind chronological age; prepubertal or early pubertal examination (testicular volume <4 mL at age 13 is consistent); family history of 'late bloomer' puberty in a parent or sibling; normal body proportions; and eventually a late but adequate pubertal growth spurt reaching near mid-parental target height. Growth velocity is typically low-normal (~4 cm/year), not frankly subnormal. IGF-1 may be low for chronological age but appropriate for bone age. CDGP requires observation and reassurance — not immediate GH therapy — unless velocity is genuinely poor or psychosocial distress is severe.
keyPoints: Commonest cause of short + delayed puberty in adolescent boys.
Delayed BA (≥2 yr), prepubertal/early puberty at 13y, family late bloomers.
Parallel low curve, velocity ~4 cm/yr — observe; don't rush GH.

items.title: How true growth hormone deficiency differs from CDGP at age 13
items.content: True GHD reflects inadequate GH secretion from the pituitary — congenital (midline defects, MPHD, genetic) or acquired (tumour, trauma, radiation). Red flags beyond poor velocity: height crossing centiles downward; severe short stature (<−3 SD); midline craniofacial anomalies; history of neonatal hypoglycaemia, prolonged jaundice, or micropenis; acquired pituitary insult; and IGF-1/IGFBP-3 consistently low for age AND bone age. On examination, disproportionate fat distribution (central adiposity) may appear in frank GHD. Bone age is delayed in both CDGP and GHD — so bone age alone cannot separate them. The discriminating combination is: decelerating velocity + low IGF-1 for bone age + failed primed GH stimulation test. Starting GH without this triad risks treating a normal variant.
keyPoints: GHD: decelerating velocity, IGF-1 low for bone age, failed primed GHST.
Red flags: centile crossing, midline defects, neonatal hypoglycaemia, MPHD.
BA delayed in both CDGP and GHD — velocity + IGF-1 + GHST discriminate.

items.title: Why mid-parental target height reframes the urgency of intervention
items.content: Calculate mid-parental (target) height: for a boy, (father's height + mother's height + 13 cm) ÷ 2, with a ±8 cm genetic range. Plot the target on the growth chart. If Anant's current height SDS is tracking toward the lower end of his target range with delayed bone age, CDGP is likely and adult height may be acceptable without GH. If he is far below target with poor velocity and no catch-up expected, intervention is more justified. Target height also contextualises familial short stature: if both parents are short, the child may simply be genetic — with normal velocity and bone age matching chronological age. Target height is the anchor for the 'watchful waiting vs treat' decision.
keyPoints: Boy target height = (FH + MH + 13) / 2 ± 8 cm.
Tracking toward target + delayed BA → CDGP likely; far below target + poor GV → investigate/treat.
Short parents → consider familial short stature if BA and velocity normal.

items.title: How bone age contributes — and what it cannot do alone
items.content: Left hand/wrist X-ray for bone age (Greulich-Pyle) estimates skeletal maturity and remaining growth potential. Delayed bone age (2+ years behind chronological age at 13) supports CDGP or endocrine delay and predicts additional growth years — a boy with BA 11 at chronological age 13 may grow into his late teens. However, bone age is also delayed in GHD, hypothyroidism, and malnutrition — it does not discriminate CDGP from GHD. Advanced bone age would suggest precocious puberty or hyperandrogenism (unlikely here). Use bone age to: (1) calculate predicted adult height; (2) interpret IGF-1 (compare to bone age, not chronological age); (3) time pubertal expectations. Order bone age early in Anant's work-up.
keyPoints: Delayed BA (≥2 yr) → more growth years; supports CDGP or endocrine delay.
BA delayed in both CDGP and GHD — cannot discriminate alone.
Use BA to interpret IGF-1 and predict adult height.

items.title: Why Tanner staging and testicular volume are mandatory at age 13
items.content: At 13 years, pubertal status determines both the diagnostic fork and the testing protocol. Document Tanner genital (G) and pubic hair (P) staging and testicular volume (orchidometer). Prepubertal testes (<4 mL) at age 13 define delayed puberty — common in CDGP, also seen in GHD and hypogonadotropic hypogonadism. The pubertal stage determines whether sex-steroid priming is required before GH stimulation testing: peripubertal or prepubertal boys at pubertal age who undergo unprimed GHST have a high false-positive rate (see cr-018). Also assess for dysmorphism, goitre, and body proportions (U:L ratio, arm span — cr-029) to exclude Turner (not applicable), skeletal dysplasia, and systemic disease.
keyPoints: Tanner G/P + testicular volume at every visit — <4 mL at 13y = delayed puberty.
Pubertal status determines GHST priming requirement.
Proportions, dysmorphism, goitre — exclude non-endocrine causes.

items.title: How to structure the initial laboratory and screening work-up
items.content: First-line screen (before GHST): TSH and free T4 (acquired hypothyroidism slows growth — treatable); IGF-1 and IGFBP-3 (low suggests GHD but must be interpreted against bone age — low-normal for BA may be normal in CDGP); celiac serology (tTG IgA ± total IgA — isolated short stature can be the only sign); CBC, ESR/CRP (chronic inflammation); basic metabolic panel. Karyotype is not indicated in a phenotypic male unless atypical features. If IGF-1 is low for bone age and velocity is poor, proceed to GH stimulation testing with sex-steroid priming. If IGF-1 is normal for bone age and velocity is ~4 cm/year with delayed BA, observe before testing.
keyPoints: TSH, IGF-1/IGFBP-3 (interpret vs bone age), celiac screen, CBC/ESR.
Low IGF-1 for BA + poor velocity → primed GHST.
Normal IGF-1 for BA + parallel velocity → observe first.

items.title: Why sex-steroid priming is mandatory before GHST in a 13-year-old boy
items.content: The GH stimulation test (GHST) provokes GH release with agents such as clonidine, glucagon, arginine, or insulin tolerance test. A peak GH below the assay-specific cutoff (commonly 7–10 ng/mL depending on laboratory) suggests GHD. Critical pitfall at Anant's age: unprimed peripubertal GHST has a high false-positive rate because somatotrophs need ambient sex-steroid tone to respond fully. Without priming, a normal boy in early puberty can 'fail' with peak GH ~3 ng/mL and be mislabelled GHD (cr-018: unprimed 3.09 → primed 23.0 ng/mL). Boys at pubertal age should receive sex-steroid priming before GHST — typically intramuscular testosterone enanthate (e.g. 50–100 mg) 1 week before, or oral estrogen protocol per local guideline. Only a failed PRIMED test, combined with low IGF-1 and poor velocity, confirms GHD.
keyPoints: Unprimed GHST at pubertal age → high false-positive GHD rate.
Prime boys with testosterone (or per protocol) before GHST.
GHD diagnosis: poor velocity + low IGF-1 for BA + failed PRIMED GHST.

items.title: How to run the structured observation period before committing to GH therapy
items.content: When CDGP is the leading diagnosis (parallel low centile, delayed BA, prepubertal at 13y, velocity ~4 cm/yr, IGF-1 normal for bone age), a structured observation period of 6–12 months is appropriate before GHST or GH therapy. Protocol: measure height with stadiometer every 3–4 months; plot velocity at each visit; repeat bone age at 12 months if puberty has not started; reassess Tanner staging and testicular volume; document psychosocial impact (bullying, school distress). If velocity remains ≥4 cm/year and puberty begins spontaneously, CDGP is confirmed and GH is not needed. If velocity falls below 4 cm/year (especially ≤3 cm/year) or puberty fails to begin by age 14, proceed to primed GHST and consider puberty induction. This observation period prevents overtreatment of normal variants.
keyPoints: CDGP likely → observe 6–12 months with heights every 3–4 months.
Velocity ≥4 cm/yr + spontaneous puberty → CDGP confirmed, no GH.
Velocity <4 (especially ≤3) or no puberty by 14y → primed GHST ± puberty induction.

items.title: When to initiate growth hormone therapy — criteria and cautions
items.content: GH therapy is indicated for confirmed GHD: persistently poor growth velocity, low IGF-1 for bone age, and failed primed GHST (two stimulants recommended by most guidelines). Additional indications may include idiopathic short stature in some jurisdictions — but this is distinct from CDGP and requires careful counselling. Before starting GH: MRI pituitary (congenital GHD, rule out tumour); secure thyroid and adrenal axes if MPHD suspected; baseline ophthalmology if risk factors. Dose ~0.025–0.035 mg/kg/day subcutaneous; monitor IGF-1 target mid-normal, growth velocity response every 3–6 months, thyroid function, and fasting glucose. Do NOT start GH based on a single unprimed low GH peak, low IGF-1 alone, or parental anxiety without poor velocity documentation.
keyPoints: GH indicated: poor velocity + low IGF-1 for BA + failed primed GHST (2 stimulants).
MRI before start; monitor IGF-1, velocity, thyroid, glucose on therapy.
Never start on unprimed GHST alone or IGF-1 alone without velocity data.

items.title: Why familial short stature must be excluded before labelling CDGP or GHD
items.content: Familial (genetic) short stature produces a healthy child who is short because short parents transmitted short stature — not delayed maturation or pituitary failure. Discriminators: height SDS parallel to mid-parental target; normal growth velocity (~5 cm/yr in childhood); bone age equal to chronological age (not delayed); normal pubertal timing; normal IGF-1. No GH therapy is indicated — the child will reach an adult height within the familial range. Take accurate parental heights and plot target. If Anant's parents are both short and his bone age matches his age with normal velocity, familial short stature is the diagnosis — not CDGP (which requires delayed BA) and not GHD.
keyPoints: Short parents + parallel curve + normal BA + normal velocity = familial.
No GH therapy — adult height within familial range.
Familial has normal BA; CDGP has delayed BA — key discriminator.

items.title: How to navigate Anant's case — the stepwise decision pathway
items.content: Step 1 — Confirm and characterise: accurate height SDS, plot ≥12 months data, calculate velocity, mid-parental target height, Tanner + testicular volume, body proportions. Step 2 — Bone age + first-line labs (TSH, IGF-1/IGFBP-3 for BA, celiac, CBC). Step 3 — Branch: (A) Velocity ~4 cm/yr, delayed BA, prepubertal, IGF-1 normal for BA → CDGP likely → observe 6–12 months with 3–4 monthly heights; (B) Velocity <4 cm/yr (especially ≤3), IGF-1 low for BA → primed GHST → if failed, MRI + GH therapy; (C) Normal BA + normal velocity + short parents → familial — reassure. Step 4 — Reassess at 6 and 12 months: if velocity remains poor or puberty absent by 14y, escalate to primed GHST and consider puberty induction (testosterone) for CDGP with significant psychosocial impact.
keyPoints: Characterise → BA + labs → branch on velocity + IGF-1 + BA.
CDGP → observe; poor velocity + low IGF-1 → primed GHST → GH if failed.
Reassess 6–12 months; puberty induction if CDGP with distress/no puberty by 14y.

items.title: How to counsel the family during the observation period
items.content: Families of short adolescent boys are anxious about adult height and bullying. Counselling points: (1) Explain that CDGP is a normal variant — 'late bloomer' — and most boys with delayed bone age grow into their late teens. (2) Show the growth chart with target height range — many boys with CDGP reach acceptable adult height without treatment. (3) Explain why GH is not started immediately — unnecessary GH carries cost, daily injections, and rare risks (intracranial hypertension, scoliosis progression, insulin resistance). (4) Define the observation contract: we will measure height every 3–4 months; if velocity stays poor, we will test properly (with priming) and treat if needed. (5) Address psychosocial support if bullying is reported. Document shared decision-making.
keyPoints: CDGP = normal variant; many reach acceptable height without GH.
GH not immediate — cost, injections, rare risks; observation has a plan.
3–4 monthly heights; escalate if velocity poor; address bullying.

items.title: Short stature 13y boy — CDGP vs GHD navigation checklist
items.content: ☐ Height SDS + plot ≥12 months on appropriate chart. ☐ Growth velocity over ≥6–12 months (target ≥4 cm/yr). ☐ Mid-parental target height calculated. ☐ Tanner staging + testicular volume (orchidometer). ☐ Body proportions (U:L ratio, arm span). ☐ Bone age X-ray. ☐ TSH/free T4, IGF-1/IGFBP-3 (interpret vs bone age), celiac screen, CBC. ☐ If CDGP likely: observe 3–4 monthly heights × 6–12 months. ☐ If velocity poor + IGF-1 low for BA: sex-steroid PRIMED GHST (≥2 stimulants). ☐ If primed GHST failed: pituitary MRI → GH therapy. ☐ Never diagnose GHD on unprimed GHST alone at age 13. ☐ Reassess puberty by age 14 — induce if absent with significant distress.
keyPoints: Velocity + BA + IGF-1 (for BA) + primed GHST = diagnostic chain.
Observe CDGP; never unprimed GHST at 13y.
GH only after failed primed test + poor velocity.

items.question: Why is growth velocity more important than a single height measurement in Anant's case?
items.answer: Because a single height below the 5th percentile confirms short stature but cannot distinguish CDGP (parallel low tracking with low-normal velocity), familial short stature (parallel with normal velocity and bone age), or GHD (decelerating subnormal velocity). Growth velocity measured over 6–12 months reveals whether the child is falling off his curve (pathological) or tracking parallel at a low centile (likely CDGP or familial). Treatment decisions — especially GH therapy — should be anchored to documented poor velocity, not a single height point.
keyPoints: Single height confirms short stature, not the cause.
Velocity distinguishes parallel (CDGP/familial) from decelerating (GHD/pathology).
GH decisions require documented poor velocity.

items.question: How should a fellow navigate the first 12 months of evaluation for a 13-year-old boy with height below the 5th percentile?
items.answer: Month 0: stadiometer height, height SDS, plot prior data, calculate velocity, target height, Tanner + testes, proportions, bone age, TSH, IGF-1/IGFBP-3 (for bone age), celiac screen. If velocity ~4 cm/yr with delayed BA and normal IGF-1 for BA → CDGP likely: observe with heights every 3–4 months, counsel family. If velocity <4 cm/yr with low IGF-1 for BA → sex-steroid primed GHST; if failed (two stimulants) → MRI + GH therapy. Month 6: replot velocity, reassess puberty. Month 12: if still prepubertal with poor velocity or no puberty progression → primed GHST or puberty induction. Never start GH on unprimed GHST alone.
keyPoints: Month 0: full characterisation + BA + labs → branch on velocity/IGF-1.
CDGP → 3–4 monthly observation; poor velocity → primed GHST.
12-month reassessment; puberty induction if needed by 14y.

items.stem: A 13-year-old boy has height below the 5th percentile. Over the past 12 months his growth velocity is 4.2 cm/year, bone age is 11 years, testes are 3 mL, and IGF-1 is normal for bone age. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Constitutional delay of growth and puberty — observe with serial heights
Growth hormone deficiency — start GH immediately
Familial short stature
Primary hypothyroidism
items.explanation: Velocity ~4 cm/yr (low-normal), delayed BA (11 at age 13), prepubertal testes, and normal IGF-1 for bone age fit CDGP. GH is not indicated without failed primed GHST and poor velocity. Familial SS has normal BA matching age. Hypothyroidism would show low velocity and elevated TSH.

items.stem: An unprimed GH stimulation test in this 13-year-old boy returns peak GH 3.5 ng/mL. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Repeat GHST with sex-steroid priming before labelling GH deficiency
Diagnose GHD and start GH therapy immediately
No further testing — the result confirms CDGP
Order a karyotype
items.explanation: Unprimed peripubertal GHST has a high false-positive rate. A peak of 3.5 ng/mL in a 13-year-old requires sex-steroid priming and repeat testing before diagnosing GHD. Immediate GH on unprimed result overtreats CDGP.

items.stem: Which combination best supports a diagnosis of true GHD rather than CDGP?
options: Growth velocity 2.8 cm/year, IGF-1 low for bone age, failed primed GHST
Growth velocity 4.5 cm/year, delayed bone age, prepubertal testes
Height below 5th percentile with short parents and bone age equal to chronological age
Delayed bone age alone
items.explanation: True GHD requires poor velocity (<4 cm/yr), low IGF-1 for bone age, and failed primed GHST. Option B describes CDGP. Option C is familial SS. Delayed BA alone occurs in both CDGP and GHD.

items.stem: Both parents are short (father 162 cm, mother 150 cm). The boy's height is −1.8 SD, growth velocity 5 cm/year, and bone age equals chronological age. What is the diagnosis?
options: Familial short stature — reassurance, no GH therapy
Constitutional delay of growth and puberty
Growth hormone deficiency
Turner syndrome
items.explanation: Short parents, normal velocity, and bone age matching chronological age indicate familial (genetic) short stature. CDGP requires delayed BA. GHD requires poor velocity and biochemical confirmation. Turner syndrome affects 46,XX females.

items.stem: A boy with likely CDGP is observed for 12 months. At review, velocity has fallen to 2.9 cm/year and he remains prepubertal at age 14. What is the best next step?
options: Proceed to sex-steroid primed GHST and consider puberty induction
Continue observation for another 2 years without testing
Reassure the family — CDGP always resolves without intervention
Start GH without stimulation testing
items.explanation: Velocity 2.9 cm/yr at age 14 with persistent prepubertal state exceeds observation thresholds. Primed GHST is needed to exclude GHD; puberty induction should be considered for CDGP with delayed puberty and psychosocial impact. Continued observation or reassurance alone is inappropriate; GH without testing violates diagnostic standards.

items.stem: Why is bone age delayed in both CDGP and GHD, making it insufficient to distinguish them?
options: Both conditions reflect delayed skeletal maturation — CDGP from late HPG/GH axis activation, GHD from inadequate GH/IGF-1 — but the mechanism and velocity patterns differ
Bone age is never delayed in CDGP
Bone age is only delayed in GHD, not CDGP
Bone age measurement is unreliable in boys
items.explanation: Both CDGP and GHD delay epiphyseal maturation, so delayed BA supports endocrine delay but does not specify the cause. Discrimination requires growth velocity pattern, IGF-1 for bone age, and primed GHST.

items.statement: A growth velocity of approximately 4 cm/year in a prepubertal 13-year-old boy is consistent with constitutional delay of growth and puberty.
items.explanation: True. CDGP typically maintains low-normal parallel velocity around 4 cm/year. Velocity ≤3 cm/year suggests pathological growth failure requiring investigation.

items.statement: A 13-year-old boy can be diagnosed with GHD based on a single unprimed GH stimulation test peak below 10 ng/mL.
items.explanation: False. Unprimed GHST at pubertal age has a high false-positive rate. Sex-steroid priming is required before labelling GHD; two stimulants are recommended.

items.statement: Growth hormone therapy should be considered if growth velocity remains poor after structured observation and a failed primed GH stimulation test.
items.explanation: True. Confirmed GHD (poor velocity + low IGF-1 for bone age + failed primed GHST) is an indication for GH therapy after pituitary MRI.

items.assertion: Anant should undergo a structured observation period before GH therapy if his growth velocity is approximately 4 cm/year with delayed bone age and normal IGF-1 for bone age.
items.reason: These findings are consistent with CDGP, a normal variant that often resolves with a late pubertal growth spurt without GH treatment.
items.explanation: Both true — velocity ~4 cm/yr, delayed BA, and normal IGF-1 for BA suggest CDGP; observation prevents overtreatment. Option 0.

items.assertion: Sex-steroid priming is unnecessary before GHST in a 13-year-old prepubertal boy.
items.reason: Somatotroph GH release is enhanced by ambient sex-steroid tone, and low peripubertal estradiol/testosterone causes false-low GH peaks on unprimed testing.
items.explanation: Assertion false (priming IS necessary at pubertal age) but reason true (mechanism of false-positive unprimed tests). Option 3.

items.assertion: Familial short stature and CDGP can be distinguished by bone age.
items.reason: Familial short stature has bone age equal to chronological age, whereas CDGP has bone age delayed by 2 or more years.
items.explanation: Both true — normal BA vs delayed BA is a key discriminator between familial SS and CDGP. Option 0.

items.question: List four features that favour CDGP over GHD in a 13-year-old boy with short stature.
items.answer: (1) Growth velocity ~4 cm/year (low-normal, parallel tracking). (2) Bone age delayed ≥2 years with IGF-1 normal for bone age. (3) Prepubertal or early pubertal examination with family history of late puberty. (4) Height tracking toward mid-parental target range without centile crossing downward.

items.question: Why must sex-steroid priming precede GHST in Anant's case?
items.answer: At age 13 the somatotropic axis is entering puberty but ambient sex steroids may be too low for full somatotroph responsiveness. Unprimed GHST produces falsely low GH peaks (high false-positive GHD rate). Priming with testosterone (boys) restores sex-steroid tone so the test reflects true GH reserve — preventing unnecessary GH therapy in CDGP.

items.question: At what point during observation should the fellow escalate from watchful waiting to primed GHST?
items.answer: Escalate when: (1) growth velocity falls persistently below 4 cm/year (especially ≤3 cm/year) over two measurement periods; (2) IGF-1 is low for bone age; (3) puberty has not begun by age 14 years; or (4) at 12 months of observation if velocity was borderline and shows no improvement. Then perform sex-steroid primed GHST (two stimulants); if failed → MRI and consider GH therapy.
